title: Finite-Time Optimal Control by Noisy Traps
authors: Luca Cocconi, Henry Alston, Thibault Bertrand
abstract: The optimal control of passive systems in equilibrium typically favours quasistatic (infinite-time) protocols. We show that a breakdown of quasistatic optimality occurs when the controller itself is dissipative. Concretely, we study a Brownian particle confined by a harmonic trap with stochastically fluctuating stiffness, driven by an external protocol. When these fluctuations violate detailed balance, the probe-controller coupling continuously exchanges work with the system, altering the optimisation landscape. In this regime, optimal protocols are characterised by a finite duration which vanishes above a critical fluctuation strength. This transition can be directly observed in a short-time expansion of the mean work functional. When imposing an endpoint constraint, the transition to zero duration disappears and finite duration protocols remain optimal for all values of the controller fluctuations. These results demonstrate that finite-time optimality can emerge in passive systems under nonequilibrium control.
summary: This paper demonstrates that when a controller is dissipative and violates detailed balance, the traditional preference for infinitely slow (quasistatic) protocols breaks down. It identifies a phase transition where the optimal control duration vanishes above a certain noise threshold. This provides new insights into how non-equilibrium fluctuations in control parameters can fundamentally alter the landscape of optimal transport.
main_problem: Investigating whether finite-time optimal control is required in passive systems when the controller itself is dissipative and violates detailed balance.
main_result: The study reveals a breakdown of quasistatic optimality where the optimal protocol duration collapses to zero above a critical fluctuation strength in unconstrained transport, though endpoint constraints prevent this collapse.
method: The authors use a variational approach with Euler-Lagrange equations, a Fokker-Planck derivation for joint probability density, and small-T expansions to identify phase transitions.
model_or_system: A passive Brownian particle in a harmonic trap where the trap stiffness fluctuates stochastically according to a continuous-time Markov chain, representing a dissipative controller.
key_observables: Mean work (cost functional), optimal protocol duration (T*), and dissipated work per unit distance.
important_parameters: Stiffness fluctuation variance (sigma_k^2), critical variance threshold (sigma_k,c^2), transport velocity (v), and the fast-switching regime.
main_assumptions: The stiffness dynamics are modeled as a Markov chain; the system is in the overdamped Langevin regime; and the fast-switching approximation is used to analyze the stiffness dynamics.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows a schematic of a particle in a noisy harmonic trap; Fig 2 illustrates the transition of optimal duration toward zero at a critical noise level and work vs. velocity plots.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of dissipative control, defines the stochastic model and Langevin dynamics, applies a variational method and multiscale expansion, analyzes the zero-time transition under different constraints, and concludes with implications for steady dragging.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: Schematic representation of a passive probe controlled by a noisy harmonic trap, here visualised as an intermittent optical tweezer. In the transport scenario, a deterministic pro- tocol translates the trap centre along the coordinate x, while the trap stiffness k(t) fluctuates stochastically and continuously exchanges work with the probe.

Fig. 2: Optimal transport with a noisy trap under steady drag- ging. (a) Work per unit distance in an intermittent trap ob- tained from Eq. (12). Here, D = 1, κ = 1 and r = 1. (b) Op- timal protocol duration for unconstrained [Eq. (22)] and con- strained [Eq. (27)] optimal transport, showing the transition to zero-time optimality. Here, D = 1, ¯k = 1 and λT = 1.

Fig. 3: Optimal stiffening with a noisy trap. (a) Mean work as a function of the optimisation parameters (c, T), showing convergence in the large-c regime. (b) Optimal protocol du- ration for unconstrained [Eq. (43)] and constrained [Eq. (47)] optimal stiffening, showing the transition to zero-time opti- mality at σk,c = (λT −λ0)/2. Here, D = 1, λ0 = 1, λT = 3 and ϵ = 10−10.
